summary:
Hello World

A sample go program is show here.

package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
  message := greetMe("world")
  fmt.Println(message)
}

func greetMe(name string) string {
  return "Hello, " + name + "!"
}

Run the program as below:

$ go run hello.go
Variables

Normal Declaration:

var msg string
msg = "Hello"

Shortcut:

msg := "Hello"
Constants
const Phi = 1.618

contents: Condition if day == "sunday" || day == "saturday" { rest() } else if day == "monday" && isTired() { groan() } else { work() } if _, err := doThing(); err != nil { fmt.Println("Uh oh") Switch switch day { case "sunday": // cases don't "fall through" by default! fallthrough case "saturday": rest() default: work() } Loop for count := 0; count <= 10; count++ { fmt.Println("My counter is at", count) } entry := []string{"Jack","John","Jones"} for i, val := range entry { fmt.Printf("At position %d, the character %s is present\n", i, val) n := 0 x := 42 for n != x { n := guess() }

contents: Variable NAME="John" echo $NAME echo "$NAME" echo "${NAME} Condition if [[ -z "$string" ]]; then echo "String is empty" elif [[ -n "$string" ]]; then echo "String is not empty" fi

Condition
if [[ -z "$string" ]]; then
  echo "String is empty"

contents:
For a star, its composition, velocity, temperature, age, etc. can all be determined by spectroscopy.
From the origin Ancient Greek and Arabian astronomers were the first to notice that the seven-colored rainbow was preceded by sunlight, but the first person to give a correct explanation for this was Isaac Newton (1642-1722). He divided light into seven colors through a prism: red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo and violet.
However, due to the limitations of optical instruments, Newton obtained the spectral resolution (resolution) is too low, to put it bluntly, the resolution is not enough, the eye is limited to the discovery of the seven colors of light, a lot of information in the refraction of light is lost.
A century later, Fraunhofer (Joseph Fraunhofer1787-1826) through the improvement of optical instruments, grating, telescopes and prisms with the extremely clear spectrum. At this time he noticed that many dark lines appeared on the clear spectrum.
We now call this spectrum the absorption spectrum, and the exact reason for this implicates another physicist.
Gustav Kirchhoff (1824-1887) discovered the secret of spectral darkening in his laboratory. When a beam of light passes through an element (such as hydrogen), the element absorbs a particular wavelength of light, which causes the light to pass through and become “incomplete,” and this is the source of the dark lines. A poor analogy would be to compare the absorption spectrum to the fingerprints of the elements. By looking at the composition and relative position (in this case relative position) of the dark lines in the spectrum, we can see what is around the light source.
Here is a description of the journey of light. A nuclear reaction in the core of the star emits a huge amount of heat, which heats up the surrounding gas, and this heat produces light, which is lost in all wavelengths. The light then travels through the interior of the star to the surface, where it is partially absorbed by elements such as hydrogen and helium along the way (leaving a fingerprint), and finally travels through the vastness of the cosmos to reach our observation point. We analyze this starlight with our instruments to obtain a spectrum, and by looking at the dark lines, the basic material composition of the star can be determined by us.
Summary The rainbow is the spectrum. By analyzing a clear spectrum we can tell what matter the star is made of, because the dark lines in the spectrum are the “fingerprints” of its constituent elements.
summary:
For a star, its composition, velocity, temperature, age, etc. can all be determined by spectroscopy.

From the origin

Ancient Greek and Arabian astronomers were the first to notice that the seven-colored rainbow was preceded by sunlight, but the first person to give a correct explanation for this was Isaac Newton (1642-1722). He divided light into seven colors through a prism: red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo and violet.

However, due to the limitations of optical instruments, Newton obtained the spectral resolution (resolution) is too low, to put it bluntly, the resolution is not enough, the eye is limited to the discovery of the seven colors of light, a lot of information in the refraction of light is lost.
title: Astronomical Spectroscopy 101

contents:
Dark patterns in the spectrum and the movement of stars A phenomenon needs to be introduced here first: the Doppler Effect (Doppler). This effect interprets the frequency of waves received by an observer in relation to the relative speeds of the source and the observer. An example of this in life is that a honking train coming toward you seems to have a higher pitch than a train moving away from you. This is because the train is chasing its own sound waves as it comes in, while at the same time emitting them, so the receiver hears them at a higher frequency, and the pitch of the departing train changes in the same way.
The Doppler effect is common to waves, so it can also be applied to light waves. When a star moves away from us, the frequency of the light it emits decreases as a whole, which causes the spectrum to shift in the direction of red light (the opposite direction of blue light) as a whole, the technical term for this is redshift. Here’s an example: if Sirius is blue and stationary relative to us, then as Sirius flies away from us, its color will shift from blue to red on the spectrum. If it is going fast enough it will show red. Similarly if the star is flying towards us it produces a blueshift in its spectrum.
Now we can explain why the absorption spectrum in part1 looks at the relative position of the dark lines, because the movement of the object causes an overall translation of the dark lines, and since the universe is expanding and many of the stars are moving away from us, their spectra are redshifted, so looking at the absolute position of the dark lines is never an option. The more redshifted it is, the faster the object is moving away from us. By looking at the amount of redshift we can determine the object’s radial velocity, which is the component velocity of the object’s velocity in the direction of its line with us. How to measure the complete velocity of a celestial body involves astronomical observations, which I will explain later.
Summary The redshift and blueshift of the spectrum tells us whether the star is moving away from us or toward us, and the amount of spectral shift indicates the magnitude of the velocity.
summary:
Dark patterns in the spectrum and the movement of stars

A phenomenon needs to be introduced here first: the Doppler Effect (Doppler). This effect interprets the frequency of waves received by an observer in relation to the relative speeds of the source and the observer. An example of this in life is that a honking train coming toward you seems to have a higher pitch than a train moving away from you. This is because the train is chasing its own sound waves as it comes in, while at the same time emitting them, so the receiver hears them at a higher frequency, and the pitch of the departing train changes in the same way.
title: Astronomical Spectroscopy 102

contents:
Temperature by color This was arguably the first part of astrophysics that broke my perception. Originally I thought, well, most flames are red, and pictures of the sun are red, so that means red stars should be hot, right? Actually, no, relatively hot stars are blue to white.
The Sun’s temperature is roughly 5,770 Kelvin (5,497 degrees Celsius), while the blue color of Senkaku VII (Rigel) can reach 11,000 Kelvin (10,726 degrees Celsius). This difference in color is also explained by the spectrum. The colors we see are actually the strongest kind of light in the visible spectrum of the star. What does this mean? It means that all of this light, red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, and violet, gets into our eyes, but it is of course the strongest of the visible light that our visual system recognizes. It should be noted that sometimes the sun appears red to the naked eye because the atmosphere affects the sun’s light, not because of its color.
Interested readers can look for the Sun: Sun (yellowish-white), Vega: Vega (bluish-white), Sirius: Sirius (bluish-white), and Sagittarius IV: Betelgeuse (reddish), which is located in the upper right and has recently undergone a dramatic change in brightness.
Theoretical explanation (If you are not interested, you can skip to the summary section)
First of all, let’s explain the concept of “Blackbody”. “As the name suggests, a blackbody is a black object. A perfect blackbody does not have any electromagnetic waves (understood as light) reflected from its surface, so its surface is “black”, but the blackbody itself radiates electromagnetic waves outward. For example, if you think of the human body as a blackbody, we radiate infrared light, which is called Blackbody Radiation. A star can also be considered a blackbody. So the human body and stars have something in common! (lol)
If we were to draw a statistical picture of a blackbody in terms of wavelength of light and intensity of light it would look something like this:
We can see that it resembles a mountain, with the top of the mountain being both the strongest beam of light. The four curves from the bottom to the top represent the spectra of four stars with low to high temperatures, and the value of the wavelength at which the peak is located (λ_max) shifts to the upper left as the temperature increases (shown by the curved dotted line in the figure). The hotter the star, the bluer its color. It can be seen that relatively hot stars are bluish and relatively cool stars are reddish. More theoretically, this image can be represented by the Planck Function.
$$B_\lambda(\lambda, T) = \frac{2hc^2}{\lambda^5} \frac{1}{e^{hc/kT\lambda} - 1}$$
The reader should not be intimidated by this equation, by varying the wavelength and temperature we can get a statistical graph of blackbody radiation similar to the image shown above. So just think of this equation as a formalization of the blackbody radiation curve. A closer derivation of Planck’s equation with respect to wavelength leads to an illuminating Wien’s Displacement Law:
$$\lambda_{peak} = \frac{b}{T}$$
The color-temperature relationship is then well explained: as the temperature increases T becomes larger, then the right-hand side of the equation becomes smaller, the wavelength becomes smaller, and the color of the corresponding light changes toward blue.
Integrating Planck’s equation leads to the Stefan-Boltzmann Law, which is another clear-cut solution:
$$L = A \times \sigma T^4 = 4 \pi R^2 \sigma T^4$$
A brief look at the equation shows the intensity of light on the left, R is the radius of the object on the right, and T is the temperature. So the higher the temperature the brighter the object, and the larger the radius the brighter the object. This is also an explanation for the speculation about the change in the brightness of Cepheus IV: Cepheus IV’s radius changes dramatically in old age, so the brightness changes dramatically along with it.
Summary Stars that are relatively hot (around 10,000 degrees Celsius) appear blue, and stars that are relatively cold (around 5,000 degrees Celsius) appear red
The light from a star is a mixture of colors, and what we see is only the color of the strongest light.
Theoretical explanation: the size of a star’s radius at the same temperature determines its brightness, and vice versa for the same radius, the temperature determines its brightness.

This difference in color is also explained by the spectrum. The colors we see are actually the strongest kind of light in the visible spectrum of the star. What does this mean? It means that all of this light, red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, and violet, gets into our eyes, but it is of course the strongest of the visible light that our visual system recognizes. title: Astronomical Spectroscopy 103
